subject: English
title: Extended Reading Passages with Questions – Three Days to See
instructions: Read each passage carefully. Answer the questions using information only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: Helen Keller was a famous woman who could not see or hear. Despite this, she lived a full life. She once wrote about what she would do if she were given the gift of sight for just three days.
questions: Who was Helen Keller?
What two senses did she lack?
How did she live her life?
What did she write about?
How many days of sight did she imagine having?

passages.paragraph: On the first day, Helen wanted to see the people who were kind to her. She wanted to look into the face of her teacher, Anne Sullivan. She wanted to see the strength and patience in her teacher's eyes.
questions: Whom did Helen want to see on the first day?
What was her teacher's name?
Where did she want to look?
What two qualities did she hope to see in her teacher's eyes?
Was Anne Sullivan kind to her?

passages.paragraph: Helen also wanted to look at the small things in her home. She wanted to see the warm colors of the rugs and the pictures on the walls. To her, these simple things made a house feel like a home.
questions: Where did Helen want to look for small things?
What part of the rugs did she want to see?
What was on the walls?
What did these simple things do for her?
Did she value simple objects?

passages.paragraph: On the second day, Helen planned to wake up early to see the dawn. She wanted to watch the sun rise and change the night into day. She thought the transformation of the sky was a magnificent miracle.
questions: When did Helen plan to wake up on the second day?
What did she want to watch?
What does the sun change the night into?
What did she call the transformation of the sky?
Did she think the sunrise was a miracle?

passages.paragraph: She also wanted to visit museums on the second day. She wanted to see the history of the world through art and statues. Even though she could feel statues with her hands, she longed to see them with her eyes.
questions: Where did she want to go on the second day?
How did she want to see the history of the world?
What had she used before to 'feel' statues?
What did she long to use to see the statues now?
What two types of items did she mention seeing at the museum?

passages.paragraph: On the third day, Helen wanted to spend time in the busy city. She wanted to see the people walking to work and the children playing. She wanted to see the expressions of joy and sadness on their faces.
questions: Where did Helen want to go on the third day?
Whom did she want to see walking to work?
What were the children doing?
What did she want to see on people's faces?
Was the city quiet or busy?

passages.paragraph: Helen observed that people with sight often see very little. They take the beauty of the world for granted. She believed that if people lost their sight for a while, they would appreciate it more.
questions: What did Helen observe about people with sight?
What do people take for granted?
What did she believe would happen if people lost their sight for a while?
Do sighted people always see everything clearly according to Helen?
What quality did she think people would gain after losing sight temporarily?

passages.paragraph: She encouraged everyone to use their eyes as if they would be struck blind tomorrow. She wanted people to notice the colors of a flower and the shape of a leaf. Every detail was precious to her.
questions: What was Helen's advice on how to use our eyes?
What should people notice about a flower?
What should people notice about a leaf?
How did she describe every detail?
Did she think we should wait until tomorrow to look closely?

passages.paragraph: As the third day ended, the darkness would return to Helen. She would not be sad because her mind would be full of beautiful memories. She would have seen enough to keep her happy for a long time.
questions: What would happen when the third day ended?
Would Helen be sad?
What would her mind be full of?
How long would these memories keep her happy?
Did she feel she would have seen enough?

passages.paragraph: The story of Helen Keller teaches us to value our senses. We should be thankful for the ability to see the world around us. Life is full of beauty if we only take the time to look.
questions: What does the story teach us to value?
What ability should we be thankful for?
What is life full of?
What must we do to find the beauty in life?
Is the lesson focused on being thankful?
